x.name: Peter Yang
x.bio: Practical AI tutorials and interviews for busy people | Join 150K+ readers at https://t.co/XYKTmGVH14

tweets.text:
Here’s the actual skill I use to set up my personal AI advisor.

I like to keep the SKILL md concise and clean so that I can share it with others. It tells Claude Code or Codex:

→ What role to play
→ Which context files to read
→ How to give advice
→ When to save new learnings

But the real magic with an advisor skill is the personal context you give it. That lives in files like:

→ plan md: Goals, principles, energy, life
→ learnings md: Patterns from past chats
→ eval md: A checklist for AI to give better advice

The more context your advisor has, the less generic the advice gets.

📌 Full tutorial: https://t.co/V5e2RCIinT
Weird that Codex imagegen can generate images but can't save those images into a local folder? https://t.co/KYGcODAc7u

x.name: Nan Yu
x.bio: head of product @linear

tweets.text:
It took us a lot of iterations to find the right pattern for this. Early on we were very naive and we thought that the agent could one shot project updates without much input from users.

That approach ended up creating a bunch of slop, because users disengaged their brain from the process of writing an update.

By switching to a multi turn interactive mode, the agent prompts you and asks you what to emphasize, what's most important, what context is missing, etc.

This simple change elicits much better project updates and ensures that the user has a chance to steer rather than simply being asked to say yes to everything.

A very good example of introducing friction for a net benefit.

it is making the right choice from a range of plausible options along many dimensions with limited information... when a chess or go player intuits a move without calculating, they are exercising taste

https://t.co/044iRz1wQf

x.name: Matt Turck
x.bio: VC at @FirstMarkCap.  Host: MAD Podcast; Organizer: Data Driven NYC, Author: MAD Landscape.

tweets.text:
This awesome conversation with @stephenbalaban of @LambdaAPI is also available on Spotify, Apple Podcasts and here on YouTube:

https://t.co/6ic2AingiG

State of AI compute 2026: my conversation with @stephenbalaban of @LambdaAPI on the neocloud boom, data centers, GPUs and what's ahead

00:00 — Cold open
01:21 — Why GPU compute was never a commodity
02:45 — The H100 price index and what it gets wrong
04:02 — The real moat: technology or financing?
05:57 — Winner-take-all, or room for many neoclouds
06:48 — Are we overbuilding or underbuilding AI compute?
09:26 — What if AI gets 10x more compute-efficient?
10:44 — The real bottleneck: land, power, and shell
11:38 — The backlash against data centers — and the misinformation
15:00 — Opening the hood: from photons to tokens
17:11 — Extracting more value from the same chip
19:26 — Frontier inference and distributed training, explained
23:26 — What actually drives compute cost
25:21 — Lambda's chip stack and the NVIDIA relationship
26:17 — A multi-silicon world? CUDA, CUDNN, and NVIDIA's real moat
28:59 — Networking, storage, and the one-click cluster
34:46 — Renting vs. owning, and full vertical integration
36:24 — How global is Lambda? Does location still matter?
38:44 — The financing stack: off-take agreements, SPVs, and credit
41:16 — Why a 2023 GPU leases for more today
42:36 — A futures market for compute?
43:54 — Origin story: facial recognition, Perceptio, and Apple
47:03 — The Lambda hat and Dream Scope
48:59 — The $60K bet that became a cloud business
52:00 — Holding the team together through the hard times
54:30 — Bringing on a new CEO; Stephen as CTO
57:33 — Matching xAI on high-velocity deployment
59:29 — "AI won't write software — it will become the software"
01:01:30 — Neural software vs. vibe coding
01:04:25 — Do agents change the compute layer
01:06:14 — Self-assembling software inside Lambda
01:08:18 — Gigawatt-scale AI factories
01:08:57 — One person, one GPU
01:12:04 — Hot takes: overrated and underrated in AI

x.name: Perplexity
x.bio: Curiosity changes everything. Download our free app on iOS, Mac, Windows, and Android.

tweets.text:
Every memory links back to the session, file, or source it came from with full transparency and control.

You can access Brain and your saved memories under "Customize" in the sidebar.

Read more about Brain: https://t.co/lExdtp176d

With Brain, Computer starts each task with full context of your projects, decisions, and sources instead of from scratch.

On tasks that require past context, Brain improves answer correctness by 25%, recall by 16%, and runs 13% cheaper per task. https://t.co/xlNHTmA1My

Introducing Brain in Computer.

Brain is a continuously learning memory system. Every task on Computer plugs into a context graph built by Brain.

It makes Computer more stateful with every run.

Available as a research preview for all Perplexity Max subscribers. https://t.co/Dw4Q7Izmqs

x.name: Cognition (Devin)
x.bio: Makers of Devin, the first AI software engineer. We are an applied AI lab building end-to-end software agents. Join us: https://t.co/JZDd4VhMfh

tweets.text:
Every PR you open in Devin Review now gets a security review. 

Try it on your next one: https://t.co/f0nc5Qbfje

Each finding is ranked by severity, tagged with a CWE ID, and comes with a fix drafted as a merge-ready pull request. Devin reviews every PR automatically, so your security posture finally keeps pace with your shipping velocity.

Learn more: https://t.co/8q7h1EgQZx

Devin reasons across your whole codebase, so it catches business-logic flaws and chained findings that only surface when you understand how the app actually works.

For example, Devin can catch a password-change endpoint that lets an unauthenticated request reset any user's password. The flaw only appears when you trace how the request moves through the auth layer, making it invisible to a pattern-matching tool.
